blurb: A low, tough wild tulip whose starry yellow-and-white flowers open flat in the sun — and, unlike the big hybrids, it actually comes back and naturalizes in our dry-summer climate.
water: Low; water in spring growth, then keep dry through summer dormancy
spread: Naturalizing; multiplies into clumps and self-sows where summers are dry, but stays well-behaved
seasons: Star-shaped yellow flowers with white tips, opening flat in sun, early-to-mid spring; narrow strappy leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when flowers open flat in sunshine
deer: Not reliably deer-resistant — like other tulips it can be browsed by deer and rabbits and dug by rodents; the small bulbs are less of a target than big hybrids
toxic: Toxic — all parts contain tulipalin (tulipalin A and B) and glycosides, like all tulips; ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, with the bulb most concentrated; handling bulbs can cause 'tulip fingers'
habitat: Stony alpine slopes & steppe with hot, dry summers

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3a–8b — fully cold-hardy in Boulder and arguably better suited to the Front Range than hybrid tulips, because it actually persists.[1][3] Species (botanical) tulips like T. tarda naturalize and multiply over years in gardens, where large border hybrids decline after the first season; our hot, dry summers mimic its native steppe and let it perennialize.[3]
care.planting: Plant the small bulbs in fall, mid-September through late October, before the ground freezes, so they root over winter.[2] Set them pointed-end up about 2–3 times the bulb's height deep — roughly 3–5 in. for these small bulbs — and 3–4 in. apart in a hot, sharply drained spot (a rock garden, gravel bed, the edge of a path, a sunny slope).[2][3] Boulder's clay holds winter and summer moisture; amend the planting pocket with coarse grit and raise the bed if water lingers, since wet soil rots the bulbs. In containers, use a gritty fast-draining mix in a terracotta pot and overwinter it in an unheated garage or cold frame to buffer freeze-thaw; pot bulbs are less cold-protected than in-ground ones.[2]
care.sun: Full sun — it needs sun both to flower well and for the blooms to open; the star-shaped flowers spread flat in bright sun and close in cloud and at night. A baking, open site is ideal.[1][3]
care.soil: Sharply drained, lean soil — sandy, gravelly, or grit-amended clay; it resents rich, wet, heavy ground and rots in it.[1][3] Neutral to alkaline suits it, so Boulder's pH is fine; concentrate on drainage rather than fertility.[3]
care.water: Low. Water in at planting and again if fall is dry. Spring rain and snowmelt usually carry it through bloom; supplement only in a warm dry spell. Once foliage yellows in early summer, stop watering and let it bake bone-dry through dormancy — the summer drought is exactly what lets this species perennialize and is essential to prevent rot.[1][3]
care.spacing: Space bulbs about 3–4 in. apart, in clusters or drifts; left undisturbed in a dry, sunny site they fill in and form colonies over a few years.[1][3]
care.propagation: By offsets: established clumps split into daughter bulbs; lift in summer dormancy if a clump gets crowded, separate the bulblets, and replant immediately at depth — or simply leave them to multiply in place. By seed: T. tarda will self-sow where summers are dry; let a few pods ripen and shed, or collect and sow seed in a gritty mix in a cold frame, expecting several years (typically 4–6) to first bloom. Letting it self-sow and multiply naturally is the easiest route and how it naturalizes.[1][3]
care.depth: Plant the small bulbs about 2–3 times their height deep — roughly 3–5 in. — slightly deeper in light sandy soil.[2][3]
care.bloom: Early-to-mid spring on the Front Range, typically April; the flat-opening yellow-and-white stars come a touch earlier than tall border tulips and last one to two weeks.[1][3]
care.feeding: Little needed — lean conditions suit it. A light scatter of bone meal or low-nitrogen bulb fertilizer at fall planting is plenty; avoid rich feeding, which favors floppy leaves over flowers and can shorten the bulb's life.[3]
care.maintenance: Nearly hands-off. Let the leaves yellow and die back fully before removing them, so they recharge the bulb. Deadhead if you want to limit self-sowing, or leave a few pods to let it naturalize. Clumps can go years without lifting; divide only when crowding reduces flowering.[2][3]
care.troubles: Bulb rot in wet, poorly drained, or summer-watered soil is the main risk — drainage and summer dryness prevent it.[1][3] Voles, squirrels, and chipmunks may dig the bulbs and deer/rabbits browse the flowers, though the small bulbs are a lesser target than big hybrids; mesh or a grit collar protects new plantings. Tulip fire (Botrytis) can spot foliage in cool wet springs.[1]
care.companions: Pair with other lean, sun-loving rock-garden and xeric plants — creeping thyme, sedum, pasque flower, dwarf iris, snow crocus, and ice plant — that share its full-sun, sharp-drainage, dry-summer needs and won't shade or overwater it.[3]

edible.summary: Do not eat any part — like all tulips, every part is toxic (the bulb most of all) and handling bulbs can irritate skin.[1][4]
edible.caution: Tulipa tarda, like all tulips, contains tulipalin A and B and glycosides in every part, most concentrated in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes vomiting, hypersalivation, depression, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, and stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting in people.[1][4] Never eat an unlabeled bulb — tulip bulbs can be mistaken for onions. Handling bulbs can cause an allergic contact dermatitis ('tulip fingers'); wear gloves. Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets; if ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]
